ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken dish — curry/stew preparation, spiced tomato-cream sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Chicken Tikka Masala (Spicy); A0CKN (chicken dish) used as the closest defensible parent node. Spicy variant noted in description.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chicken Tikka Masala as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Chicken Tikka Masala is approximately 350 g, typically comprising grilled chicken pieces in spiced tomato-cream gravy, served with naan or rice on the side.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken Curry / Chicken Tikka Masala composite; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Chicken curry, home-prepared, ID 174591)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides the primary Indian-cuisine anchor for chicken curry preparations. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken curry (ID 174591) was used to cross-validate macros and micronutrients. Values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where they fall within the plausible range confirmed by both sources. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not reported in ICMR-NIN for this preparation; set to -1. Potassium estimated at 320 mg/100g based on ingredient composition (tomatoes, onions, and chicken) consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for chicken curry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on chicken curry with tomato-cream sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Tikka Masala (Spicy) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: chicken curry with tomato-based sauce (low-GI category, GI ~40–50), consistent with the dish's low carbohydrate content (5.71 g/100g), high protein (12.86 g/100g), and fat content (10.86 g/100g) which collectively blunt postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 19.985) / 100 = 9.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 271 mg/100g; estimated 949 mg per 350 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 271 mg/100g is borderline elevated; a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 949 mg sodium, representing 41% of the WHO daily limit of 2,300 mg — patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted pappadums, and limit portion size to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.71 g/100g), high protein (12.86 g/100g), estimated GI ~45
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (5.71 g/100g), high protein (12.86 g/100g), and estimated GI of 45 make this dish suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white naan or white rice; patients should choose wholemeal bread or a small portion of brown rice and request reduced cream to limit saturated fat that may worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.14 g/100g; cholesterol 51.43 mg/100g; sodium 271 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 5.14 g/100g (from ghee and cream) marginally exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold relevant to cardiovascular risk; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 18 g saturated fat and 180 mg cholesterol — patients with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with reduced ghee and cream, substitute ghee with extra-virgin olive oil where possible, and limit portion to 150–200 g to keep saturated fat intake within daily targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.14 g/100g exceeds 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; nightshade vegetables present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 5.14 g/100g triggers pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced ghee and cream to bring saturated fat below 5 g/100g, monitor symptoms if sensitive to nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, green chillies), and consider substituting ghee with extra-virgin olive oil to improve the anti-inflammatory profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Tikka Masala (Spicy) is a popular Indian curry dish consisting of boneless chicken pieces that are first marinated in spiced yogurt and grilled in a tandoor oven to achieve a characteristic smoky char, then simmered in a rich, spicy tomato-cream gravy seasoned with garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, ginger, garlic, and fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi). The dish is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia, typically accompanied by naan bread or basmati rice. The texture is tender and juicy with a velvety, moderately thick sauce; the flavour profile is complex — smoky, tangy from tomatoes, mildly sweet from cream, and distinctly spicy from chilli and garam masala. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 166 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 10.9 g fat (5.1 g saturated), and only 5.7 g carbohydrate, making it a relatively low-carbohydrate, protein-rich curry. Sodium is 271 mg/100g, which is borderline elevated. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 45 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when paired with low-GI accompaniments. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to low carbohydrate and high protein content, but requires caution for hypertension (sodium load per serving) and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from ghee and cream). Anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric and ginger provide bioactive benefits, though saturated fat marginally exceeds the threshold relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

ingredient_synonyms.cream: heavy cream, cooking cream, coconut cream (dairy-free variant)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ai_generated input (confidence 0.7) cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Indian Foods data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken curry entry. Values fall within the plausible range for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are unavailable from the primary source. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chicken Tikka Masala are not reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken curry entry as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific spicy preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy at the level of detail required. Potassium estimated at 320 mg/100g from ingredient composition (tomatoes, onions, and chicken) consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for chicken curry; this is an estimate and should be confirmed by laboratory analysis. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: 350 g serving × 5.71 g carb/100g = 19.985 g available carbohydrate; GL = (45 × 19.985) / 100 = 9.0. Input ethnic_tags included 'Biryani', 'Butter Chicken', and 'Naan' which are separate dishes and have been removed from ethnic_tags; they are retained as contextual ingredient synonyms in llm_training only. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302757 is the best available match for chicken tikka masala in the FoodOn ontology; match type is exact. FoodEx2 code A0CKN is used as the closest defensible parent node for a chicken dish in curry/stew preparation; match type is closest_parent as no spicy-variant-specific code exists. Q8 fix (iteration 3): previous draft contained a mixed Hindi entry 'मसालेदार चिकन टिक्ka मसाला' (with Latin 'k') in aliases_by_language.hindi that did not match any flat alias verbatim; corrected to 'मसालेदार चिकन टिक्का मसाला' and verified all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Romanized entries 'Murgh Tikka Masala' and 'Murgh Tikka Masala (Teekha)' confirmed present in flat aliases array.
